participants.reasoning: The extracted participants description matches the official criteria, sample size, randomization, caregiver demographics, and patient illness characteristics with only minor rounding differences in ages.
participants.extracted: Sixty-eight family caregivers were randomly selected from a list of eligible primary carers of people with schizophrenia attending two major psychiatric outpatient clinics in Hong Kong, and 64 consented to participate. Participants were adults aged 18 years or older who could understand and read Chinese, lived with and cared for one relative with a primary DSM-IV diagnosis of schizophrenia, and whose relative had schizophrenia for three years or less without comorbid mental illness at recruitment. Exclusion criteria were caring for more than one family member with mental illness, having a mental illness themselves, or serving as primary carer for less than three months. One primary caregiver per patient was enrolled, and the final sample was randomized to either the psychoeducation and mutual support group (n=34) or routine care (n=34). The caregivers were mostly female, about 42 years old on average, with secondary education or above, and commonly the patient’s child, spouse, or parent; the patients were mostly male, around 29–37 years old, and had a mean illness duration of about 2.5 years.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official description in content, format, frequency, duration, delivery, and routine care comparison, with only minor additional detail.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was a 36-week family psychoeducation and mutual support group for primary caregivers of people with schizophrenia, delivered alongside conventional psychiatric outpatient care. It consisted of 18 biweekly two-hour sessions over nine months. The program combined didactic psychoeducation with guided group discussion and mutual aid, covering orientation to the program, understanding schizophrenia and its symptoms, etiology and course, illness behavior and its effects on family life, treatment and rehabilitation, communication and coping skills, problem solving, medication compliance, crisis management, and use of community support services. The first sessions were caregiver-only, while later sessions included family members and, in some sessions, patients. A trained psychiatric nurse led the groups, with input from psychiatrists, psychiatric nurse specialists, and medical social workers as needed. The mutual support component emphasized sharing experiences, confidentiality, commonality, reciprocal help, and practical rehearsal of caregiving strategies in the home setting. The comparison condition was routine psychiatric outpatient and family care, which included medical consultation and advice, individual nursing support, social welfare/financial counseling, and psychologist counseling if necessary.
interventions.official:
Group 1: family psychoeducation and support group (n = 34)
Content: each session began with a didactic presentation, followed by a group discussion that emphasised the significance and relevance of information obtained for their family lives. Caregiving situations and incidents were presented by the caregivers and alternative ways of coping and resolving their difficulties of care provision were discussed among the caregivers.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official list in substance, including patient functioning, rehospitalisation duration, family functioning, caregiver burden, and services utilisation, with only added timing and minor wording details.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure family burden using the Family Burden Interview Schedule (FBIS), family functioning using the Family Assessment Device (FAD), perceived use/need of family support services using the Family Support Services Index (FSSI), and patients’ level of functioning using the Specific Level of Functioning Scale (SLOF). It also planned to measure the number and duration of psychiatric hospital admissions/readmissions obtained from hospital and outpatient clinic records. Outcomes were assessed at three time points: baseline (Time 1, prior to the intervention), 1 month after the intervention began/completed (Time 2), and 12 months after the intervention (Time 3).
